sentences.text: Yes.
Yeah.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, we knew that the German army took half of Poland and the Soviet army took the other half, which was the clear part of Western Ukraine, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
We knew that the area north of us became part of Belarus.
Bialystok and all that area.
And we also knew when the-- a year later I believe when the Russians, the Soviet army, moved in on Romania and took over Bessarabia and Bukovina.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And also, Lithuania, Latvia, Estonia became part of the Soviet Union.
We also knew about Dunkirk [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] later on.
I believe it was 1940, where the British and the French had to evacuate.
We knew-- I knew about Vichy.
How did I feel?
Well, we knew that we were alive.
We felt terrible about the German army being able to blitzkrieg everybody, you know.
Czechoslovakia was the first one, actually.
How could you feel?
You heard.
We knew what the cousin-- Mother's cousin told us.
There were stories going around.
But it was hard, you know.
There were no proofs or anything substantial.
At that particular time, yes.
Between 1939 and '41, I felt safe.
When they attacked us?
Yes, positively.
Oh yes.
Not-- I wasn't there anymore.
By that time I was already in the army.
Well, first let me tell you there was bombing again on the 21st of June, 1945.
Excuse me, 1941.
I beg your pardon.
I skipped four years.
[LAUGHS] The bombing was a daily a occurrence, twice a day, three times a day.
Not the center of the city, mind you, but the roads like the railroad and so on outside the city.
The-- we knew there's a war on.
The Soviets were sending out draft notices to all the eligible men, 18 and older.
Most of them were hiding.
They did not want to join the Soviet army.
Men in-- I would say most Ukrainians-- Gentiles and Jews.
They didn't want to go who knows where.
And many of them didn't believe that the Germans would kill any civilian population.
So I was walking in the center of the city.
It was like two days into the war, two or three days into the war.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was the same height like I'm right now.

Much younger, I should say, and they had military police checking all the passports, identifications of all the men that they could spot in the-- in-- in the street to see how come you're not the army.
You know, you received a notice.
The notice came over the radio.
All men report for military duty.
And as nobody showed up, they want to know why.
[LAUGHS] So they caught me, and I was only 16.
I was 16 May 26, and this was June 21, 23, 24.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they brought me into the-- to the barracks.
I protested that I was too young.
I even showed them my passport that I had.
It didn't help.
You're old enough to fight for your country.
They were desperate to get men.
I was held in the barracks for two days, given a uniform, given a gun, and by that time, I was able-- I didn't come home.
My mother realized what was happening.
She came looking for me and she found me on the other side of the fence, which we couldn't go out.
And she went to see the commander, and he says, we-- he's now a soldier.
That's it.
You can't.
She went away crying, and that was the end, the last time I saw her.
Two days later, we were marched out retreating.
None whatsoever.
They gave us a gun.
They did show me how to use the gun.
A rifle.
One bullet each time.
It was an infantry unit.
That's it.
And as we were retreating by horse and buggy, by walking, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the strafing by the airplanes were killing hundreds of people-- soldiers and civilians.
We were retreating to the Russian border.
Actually, we were part of the Soviet Union too.
The old Soviet border.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everybody scattered, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even the military.
Everybody was on their own.
There was no discipline.
Officers were killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was a soldier, but I had no idea where I was going or what I was doing.
I was just following the crowd.
Some ran away, yes.
As I was walking, I bumped into a horse and wagon which was driven by somebody I knew in my hometown, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with his son that I knew from Kolomyja.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I hopped on the wagon, and we came across the old border.
And we stopped near a place where I wanted to buy something to-- to get something to eat.
I haven't eaten in a day and a half.
As I went in and I came out, the horse and wagon were gone.
The guy drove away without me.
So I walked for about two hours.
I came to a railroad station.
In my uniform with my gun, I hopped on the train.
There were hundreds and hundreds of soldiers from different units that hopped on the train.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were on the train for overnight, and in the morning, we wound up in Kiev.
Yes, there were others.
I was-- well-- was I treated-- there was-- you didn't associate with too many people too long.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Right at the beginning of the war, I'm talking about.
They were making remarks, yeah, [NON-ENGLISH] this and [NON-ENGLISH] that, you know.
Jew this and Jew that, some of them.
Some of them were kidding and saying a Jew shoots with a crooked rifle from around the corner.
You must have heard that expression somewhere.
They did make remarks.
But they didn't kill you.
You were part of the whole-- you were hungry.
Everybody was hungry.
You were miserable.
Everybody was miserable.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
You mean later in the war?
Yes.
Yes.
We were in different platoons, let's say, not in the same company.
So when we had a little spare time, we used to get together and talk.
I would say-- No, one of the officers was Jewish.
Oh yes, no two ways about it.
Oh yes.
Yeah.
I was promoted to sergeant.

On the field, the sergeant was killed and apparently I was the only one who knew something more than the rest of them-- how to read and write, how to speak on the telephone, how to do this, how to do that, you know.
So--
Kiev, yeah.
Well, the city was in turmoil.
I came off the train.
They had some people who told us where to go.
They-- we had-- they had nowhere to put us to sleep.
It was still summertime, mind you.
It was June, the end of June the beginning of July, and they put us up to sleep in a park, a city park.
We-- I had my coat, a roll like.
I just put it down, and I slept in the park.
No, not at all.
No.
No.
What happened?
Well, they knew what was happening.
The war was on.
The military police was rounding up all the stragglers.
They picked me up again at the park, you know, where I was sleeping.
They brought me into-- I explained to them what happened.
They put me in a different unit, a completely different unit, all Russians.
At least before there were a few people from my hometown.
Here I was all by myself, completely different.
And they sent us to the front.
The front by that time was just outside Kiev.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We didn't last there long.
Order was retreat again.
So we retreated as fast as we could.
First I walked.
Then I hopped again on the wagon with horses.
Then I was able to get on the truck, an old Russian truck.
With other soldiers.
And we retreated and we wound up all way down to Kharkiv.
I believe it's C-H-A-R-K-O-V, Kharkiv.
Well, I was-- I wasn't there too long either.
[LAUGHS] They picked us up, and they started new units again.
Because once you retreated you were on your own as far as you could go.
Many of them were captured.
You know.
Many of them were killed, captured.
I was one of the lucky few.
I got there.
Pardon?
Infantry.
I couldn't think of the word.
Anyway, it was still infantry, and they organized divisions.
They were bringing in replacements, and-- you know, from Siberia.
They were bringing in thousands of men, and they-- they were integrating the old ones and the new ones and all that stuff.
From unit to unit.
Exactly.
No.
Not at all.
Not until I got to the city of Kuibyshev, which was Samara--
--on the Volga River in 1943.
I was retreating, retreating.
I met a Jew here and a Jew there, but I was never with a Jewish community so to speak.
No.
OK.
From your experience between 1941 and 1943, how were the Jews in the military treated by the military?
I would say the same as everybody else.
They were just soldiers.
No.
There were anti-Semites.
It depends which part of the country some people came from.
If you were a Russian peasant who came from kolkhoz, or came from Siberia--
Is a-- in Hebrew a--
A Kibbutz.
Collective farm.
Yes.
If you came from-- from a kibbutz or a collective farm, you hardly-- I don't think you ever met a Jew.
They had something, some preconceived notions, what their priest used to tell them a Jew was, like a black guy with horns on his head.
But they really never met a Jew so they never knew what a Jew was.
But when you came from a big city and-- you know, where anti-Semitism had a hold or something like that, then-- then there were anti-Semites, yes.
Yes.
If you ran across an officer who had a higher-- some education, they were anti-Semitic, some of them.
Well, they had their upbringing, I guess, certain preconceived notions about what a Jew was or what he wasn't.
You couldn't tell what certain people were brought up like.
The church instilled it in them.
Yes.
First, when I was in that units between Kharkiv and Kuibishev, I was wounded, so I was sent to the hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in the hospital, like I said, they were-- I was like in any wounded soldier.
They treated me well and so on.
But instead of sending me back to the unit, they sent me to a factory where they were manufacturing airplanes.
Because they desperately needed the parts to build the airplanes for the war.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I worked there for six months, 12-hour shifts-- as a soldier mind you.
Army pay, whatever that was.
Rubles.
Well, the army paid.
Yeah, we had a paymaster in the army.
And the food was hardly any.
We also had in the same factory where we were working the 12-hour shifts, prisoners, Russian political prisoners.
Criminals they put them to work.
And they had worse than we had.
Hmm?
I couldn't tell you.
I don't know.
I don't think so.
Rounded up, yeah.
There might have been some of them.
I really couldn't tell you.
No, I wouldn't say that, no.
And, well, so they-- the food was hardly anything, a little soup.
There was nothing in it, mostly water.
And they gave us 200 grams of black bread, which was mostly sawdust.
They mixed it with sawdust to stretch it.
The prisoners, on the other hand, were getting nothing, practically.
They were starving in the cold outside.
They used them just like a concentration camp, I would say.
When we threw away the garbage, the soldiers, they used to fight for the garbage.
You couldn't.
You really couldn't do anything.
It was like a-- animals.
We-- this-- the-- their guards, you couldn't even come close to them.
They had the [INAUDIBLE] guards, you know, the secret or whatever police, which was, you know, there all the time.
Nothing.
We weren't so free either.
We were soldiers, and we had a special card with our picture on it and a number where we could get in and out.
That's all we could do.
In a barrack.
Near the factory, yes.
Only men.
No.
No.
No social life, no nothing.
I was able to go out.
When-- when I got the end of the month, I got a couple of rubles, and I was hungry.
I used to go out to the market.
The markets still had Russian peasant women selling from their farms whatever.
You know, they had their own plots, because most farms were collective farms, so they had their own plots.
So they used to make [NON-ENGLISH], they called it, like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE], but they were baked with potato or whatever and they were very expensive.
But whenever it was, I-- I blew my whole month's salary on a couple of these things so I could eat something, [LAUGHS] you know, and survive.
And you were there for six months?
Close to-- five-- five and a half months, yes.
Then the crops were coming in on the kohlkoz, which is the collective farm.
They had no men to take them off, so the men were taken off the factories and sent there.
That was September sometimes.
And we were sent to take off the food from the collective farms.
Still in the army.
Pardon?
Still the same thing.
Paid.
[LAUGHS]
So-- but instead of staying in barracks, we stayed individually with peasant families in their homes.
Each soldier or two soldiers were assigned to one family.
And besides working so many hours taking off the wheat, they were afraid that the Germans were going to break through, so they put us for hours digging trenches.
Two and a half months.
Winter.
No, I would say it was the winter-- the end of '42 the beginning of '43.
After this, I'm trying to remember.
After this, I was put into this outfit, the [? mortar ?] division.
Now this was a real organized unit.
I was already 18, and we had formal training in this unit.
We had how to handle the armaments.
I was given a sub-machine gun with 74 round bullets, you know, automatic.
We, for three, four months, we got up every-- early in the morning training-- running, jumping all over things, you know, like obstacles.
Lectures, what the fascists were doing to the country, how they were killing women and children.
They instilled a hate in us for the Germans, the Nazis, with no mercy.
They were killing our families.
No.
I was-- it was in the Nazi territory.
It was an occupied territory.
I was in-- near Kuibishev, near [INAUDIBLE].
It was completely different.
This was Soviet-dominated area.
And that was the German-dominated.
Between us was the front.
No.
What happened is after the training, they put us on a boat on the Volga River.
They brought in a lot of smaller boats, and we were shipped down the Volga River to a certain point where we landed on the Western side of the Volga River.
And we were part of the units that surrounded [INAUDIBLE].
You know, circle.
They tried to break out.
They couldn't.
We're going to pause here, Jack, to change the tape again.
